1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. 27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).

94	\begin{proof}
95	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
96	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
97	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~thm:tgtsp. By
98	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
99	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
100	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
101	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
102	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
103	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
104	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
105	
106	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
107	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
108	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
109	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
110	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
111	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
112	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
113	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
114	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
115	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
116	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
117	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
118	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
119	  \[
120	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
121	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
122	  \]
123	
124	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
125	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
126	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
127	  abelian groups
128	  \[
129	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
130	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
131	  \]
132	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
133	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
134	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
135	  \[
136	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
137	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
138	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
139	  \]
140	
141	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
142	  represents the étale-sheafification of
143	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
144	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
145	  \[
146	    \begin{array}{ccc}
147	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
148	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
149	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
150	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
151	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
152	    \end{array}
153	  \]
154	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
155	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 5, Exercise~ex:Alr;
156	  invocation legitimised by
157	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
158	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
159	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
160	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
161	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
162	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
163	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
164	
165	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
166	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
167	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
168	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
169	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
170	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
171	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
172	
173	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
174	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
175	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
176	\end{proof}

178	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
179	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
180	
181	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
182	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
183	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
184	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
185	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
186	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
187	dimension \(g\).
188

190	  \label{thm:pic0_smooth}

245	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
246	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
247	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm, with the curve-specific dimension
248	  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.
249	
250	  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
251	  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
252	  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
253	  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
254	  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
255	  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
256	  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
257	  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
258	  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
259	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
260	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
261	  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
262	  \(0\).
263	
264	  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
265	  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
266	  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
267	  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
268	  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
269	  smoothness. Hence
270	  \(
271	    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
272	  \)
273	  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
274	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).
275	
276	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
277	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
278	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~ex:jac
279	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
280	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
281	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
282	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
283	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:ch0): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
284	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
285	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
286	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
287	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
288	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
289	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
290	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
291	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
292	
293	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
294	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
295	\end{proof}

297	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
298	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
299	
300	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
301	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
302	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
303	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
304	
305	\begin{theorem}[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
306	  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
308	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
309	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}

359	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
360	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
361	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, specialised to \(X = C\) a
362	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
363	
364	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
365	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
366	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
367	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
368	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
369	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
370	  (\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, first conclusion) then gives that
371	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
372	  is projective and geometrically integral.
373	
374	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
375	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
376	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
377	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
378	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
379	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
380	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
381	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
382	
383	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
384	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
385	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
386	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
387	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
388	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
389	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
390	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
391	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
392	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
393	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
394	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
395	  is constant.
396	
397	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
398	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
399	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
400	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
401	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
402	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:cmp) \(t\) is represented by an
403	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
404	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
405	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
406	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
407	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
408	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
409	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
410	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
411	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
412	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
413	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
414	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
415	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
416	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
417	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
418	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
419	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
420	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
421	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
422	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
423	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
424	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
425	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
426	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
427	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
428	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
429	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
430	  properness.
431	\end{proof}
432	
433	\section{Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
434	\label{sec:pic0_geom_irred}
435	
436	Geometric irreducibility is delivered directly by the abstract
437	identity-component substrate
438	(\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}) applied
439	to the \(k\)-group scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\). We restate the
440	specialisation here for the assembly downstream.
441	
442	\begin{theorem}[Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
443	  \label{thm:pic0_geom_irred}
444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible}
445	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
446	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
447	
448	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0;
449	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
450	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2921-L2929).
451	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
452	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
453	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
454	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
455	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
456	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
457	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
458	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
459	  % \(k\).
460	  %  \end{prp}"
461	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0
462	  (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the
463	  identity).}
464	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
465	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
466	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is geometrically irreducible: after
467	  base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme
468	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) is irreducible.
469	\end{theorem}
470	
471	\begin{proof}
472	  \uses{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
473	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes,
474	        thm:fga_pic_representability}
475	  Apply the abstract identity-component substrate to the \(k\)-group
476	  scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), which is locally of finite type by
477	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. By
478	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}, the
479	  identity component \((\Pic_{C/k})^0 = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite
480	  type and geometrically irreducible over \(k\). The base-change
481	  compatibility of
482	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes} identifies
483	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\),
484	  closing the proof.
485	\end{proof}
486	
487	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
488	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
489	
490	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
491	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
492	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
493	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
494	identity-component group-scheme structure of
495	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
496	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
497	gate for Route~A.
498	
499	\begin{theorem}[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
500	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
502	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
503	        thm:pic0_proper,
504	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
505	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
506	
507	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
508	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
509	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
510	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
511	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
512	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
513	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
514	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
515	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
516	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
517	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
518	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
519	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
520	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
521	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
522	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
523	  % Exercise~REF."
524	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
525	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
526	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac (the Jacobian
527	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
528	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
529	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
530	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
531	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
532	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
533	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
534	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
535	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
536	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
537	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
538	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
539	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
540	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
541	\end{theorem}
542	
543	\begin{proof}
544	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
545	        thm:pic0_proper,
546	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
547	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
548	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
549	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
550	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
551	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
552	
553	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
554	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
555	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
556	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
557	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
558	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
559	
560	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
561	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
562	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
563	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
564	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
565	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
566	
567	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
568	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
569	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
570	
571	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
572	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
573	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
574	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
575	
576	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
577	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
578	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
579	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
580	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
581	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
582	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
583	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
584	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
585	
586	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac: for \(X/S\)
587	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
588	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
589	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
590	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
591	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
592	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
593	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
594	\end{proof}
595

653	\section{Out of scope}
654	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety_out_of_scope}
655	
656	This chapter packages \emph{only} the five A.3.iii--vii steps. The
657	following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters
658	and are explicitly out of scope here:
659	
660	\begin{itemize}
661	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
662	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
663	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
664	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}.
665	  \item \textbf{Autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
666	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6; Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac, Abel map) ---
667	    not needed for the Jacobian's existence as an abelian variety,
668	    hence out of scope.
669	  \item \textbf{Torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
670	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
671	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
672	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
673	    Route~A.
674	  \item \textbf{Genus-zero degenerate case.} When \(g(C) = 0\),
675	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the trivial group scheme \(\Spec k\)
676	    (Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)); this is consistent with the
677	    abelian-variety assembly (a point is trivially an abelian variety
678	    of dimension \(0\)) and is handled uniformly here, but is not
679	    separately stated.
680	\end{itemize}

10	/-!
11	# The identity component of the Picard scheme (A.3)
12	
13	This file is the **A.3** sub-build chapter for the project's positive-genus arm
14	of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It scaffolds the abstract identity-component
15	substrate for a `k`-group scheme locally of finite type and specialises it to
16	`G = Pic_{C/k}`, packaging:
17	
18	1. the open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure of the identity component,
19	2. the degree map `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`,
20	3. the abelian-variety identification of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the Jacobian variety
21	   of `C` when `C/k` is a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
22	   positive genus).
23	
24	## Status (iter-185 NEW file-skeleton)
25	
26	This file is the **iter-185 IdentityComponent** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
27	blueprint-reviewer HARD GATE cleared for the iter-184 plan-phase chapter
28	`Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned
29	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
30	`\lean{...}` pin in `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The
31	bodies are iter-186+ work; iter-185's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
32	
33	The 5 pinned declarations are:
34	
35	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
36	   **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite
37	   type, as a `k`-scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object). Abstract substrate
38	   reusable outside the Picard context; not yet in Mathlib.
39	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`
40	   (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the bundled statement that `G^0` is an open and
41	   closed subscheme of `G` via an open immersion `G^0 ↪ G`.
42	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **identity
43	   component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, obtained by applying
44	   `IdentityComponent` to `G = PicScheme C`.
45	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **degree
46	   map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`, extracting the leading coefficient of the
47	   Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed
48	   degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`.
49	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
50	   — the **abelian-variety identification** of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`: smooth, proper,
51	   geometrically irreducible `k`-group scheme of dimension `g(C)` --- the
52	   Jacobian variety of `C`.
53	
54	## Note on type expressivity
55	
56	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
57	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
58	
59	- `IdentityComponent G : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme, not the
60	  tautological `G` itself.
61	- `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme G` — asserts the *existence* of an
62	  open immersion morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` whose underlying map of
63	  schemes is both an open and a closed immersion (clopen subscheme).
64	- `Pic0Scheme C : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme.
65	- `PicScheme.degree C : (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ` —
66	  a genuine function from `k`-points to `ℤ`, not a constant.
67	- `Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety C` — asserts the conjunction of the four
68	  abelian-variety properties (proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible,
69	  group-object structure); not vacuous because each conjunct is a genuine
70	  property/structure on the (typed-sorry) `Pic0Scheme C`.

94	/-! ## §1. The identity component of a group scheme — abstract substrate
95	
96	The identity component is an abstract feature of `k`-group schemes locally of
97	finite type. We package the definition + open-and-closed-subgroup-scheme
98	structure in the `GroupScheme` namespace so they are reusable outside the
99	Picard context.
100

100	
101	In this project, a "`k`-group scheme" is encoded as an
102	`Over (Spec (.of k))`-object `G` carrying a `[GrpObj G]` instance (the
103	group-object structure in the over-category). The locally-of-finite-type
104	hypothesis is the `[LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]` instance on the structural
105	morphism to the base.
106	
107	Blueprint references: `def:identity_component_group_scheme` +
108	`thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`). -/
109	
110	namespace GroupScheme
111	
112	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, the set of
113	connected components is finite.
114	
115	Proof: the map `irreducibleComponents α → ConnectedComponents α` sending each
116	irreducible component `C` to the connected component of an arbitrary chosen
117	point of `C` is well-defined (because `C` is preconnected, hence contained in
118	a single connected component) and surjective (because every point lies in
119	some irreducible component, namely `irreducibleComponent x`, which is a
120	subset of `connectedComponent x`). The source is finite by
121	`TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents`, so the
122	target is finite by `Finite.of_surjective`. -/
123	private lemma noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
124	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α] :
125	    Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := by
126	  classical
127	  have hfin : (irreducibleComponents α).Finite :=
128	    TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents
129	  haveI : Finite ↥(irreducibleComponents α) := hfin.to_subtype
130	  refine Finite.of_surjective
131	    (fun C : ↥(irreducibleComponents α) =>
132	      (ConnectedComponents.mk : α → ConnectedComponents α)
133	        ((C.property : Maximal IsIrreducible C.val).prop.nonempty.some)) ?_
134	  intro c
135	  obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := ConnectedComponents.surjective_coe c
136	  refine ⟨⟨irreducibleComponent x, irreducibleComponent_mem_irreducibleComponents x⟩, ?_⟩
137	  rw [ConnectedComponents.coe_eq_coe]
138	  refine (connectedComponent_eq ?_).symm
139	  exact irreducibleComponent_subset_connectedComponent
140	    (isIrreducible_irreducibleComponent (x := x)).nonempty.some_mem
141	
142	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, each
143	connected component is open.
144	
145	Proof: `ConnectedComponents α` is totally disconnected
146	(`ConnectedComponents.totallyDisconnectedSpace`), hence T1
147	(`TotallyDisconnectedSpace.t1Space`), and finite by
148	`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`. A finite T1 space is discrete
149	(`Finite.instDiscreteTopology`), so the singleton
150	`{ConnectedComponents.mk x}` is open in the quotient. The preimage of this
151	singleton under the continuous quotient map `ConnectedComponents.mk` is
152	`connectedComponent x` (`connectedComponents_preimage_singleton`), which is
153	therefore open. -/
154	private lemma noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent
155	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α]
156	    (x : α) :
157	    IsOpen (connectedComponent x) := by
158	  haveI : Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
159	  haveI : DiscreteTopology (ConnectedComponents α) := inferInstance
160	  have h := (isOpen_discrete
161	    ({(ConnectedComponents.mk x : ConnectedComponents α)} : Set _)).preimage
162	    ConnectedComponents.continuous_coe
163	  rwa [connectedComponents_preimage_singleton] at h
164	
165	/-- Helper (iter-188): the **`LocallyConnectedSpace` instance** for the
166	underlying topological space of a `k`-scheme `G.left` whose structural
167	morphism `G.hom : G.left ⟶ Spec k` is locally of finite type.
168	
169	The substantive content is EGA I 6.1.9: a locally Noetherian topological
170	space has open connected components — equivalently, is locally connected.
171	Pushed through the chain: `Spec k` is Noetherian (a field has Noetherian
172	spectrum); `LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom + IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k) ⟹
173	IsLocallyNoetherian G.left` (Mathlib's
174	`AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian`); and the
175	implication `IsLocallyNoetherian X ⟹ LocallyConnectedSpace X.toTopCat`
176	is the iter-188 project-side helper (`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`
177	+ `noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent`).
178	
179	The classical proof: each point `y ∈ G.left` has an open affine
180	neighbourhood `W = Spec R` with `R` Noetherian, hence `|W|` is a Noetherian
181	topological space; in a Noetherian space, each `connectedComponent y` is
182	clopen (finite irreducible components ⟹ finite connected components ⟹
183	finite T1 quotient ⟹ discrete quotient ⟹ singletons clopen in quotient ⟹
184	preimages clopen). Pulled back along the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left`,
185	this gives an open connected neighbourhood of `y` inside any open `F`
186	containing `y`. -/
187	private instance identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace
188	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
189	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
190	    [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
191	    LocallyConnectedSpace G.left := by
192	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian G.left := LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian G.hom
193	  rw [locallyConnectedSpace_iff_subsets_isOpen_isConnected]
194	  intro x F hF
195	  -- Extract an open neighbourhood `T ⊆ F` of `x`.
196	  obtain ⟨T, hTF, hT, hxT⟩ := mem_nhds_iff.mp hF
197	  -- Find an affine open `W ⊆ T` with `x ∈ W`.
198	  obtain ⟨W, hW, hxW, hWT⟩ :=
199	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := G.left) (U := ⟨T, hT⟩) hxT
200	  haveI : IsNoetherianRing Γ(G.left, W) :=
201	    IsLocallyNoetherian.component_noetherian ⟨W, hW⟩
202	  haveI hNoethW : TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace ↥W :=
203	    noetherianSpace_of_isAffineOpen W hW
204	  -- Inside `W` (Noetherian) the connected component of `x` is open; its image
205	  -- under the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left` is the connected open neighbourhood
206	  -- we want.
207	  let xW : ↥W := ⟨x, hxW⟩
208	  refine ⟨(Subtype.val : ↥W → G.left) '' connectedComponent xW, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
209	  · rintro _ ⟨z, _, rfl⟩
210	    exact hTF (hWT z.2)
211	  · exact W.isOpen.isOpenMap_subtype_val _
212	      (noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent xW)
213	  · exact ⟨xW, mem_connectedComponent, rfl⟩
214	  · exact isConnected_connectedComponent.image _ continuous_subtype_val.continuousOn
215	
216	/-- The image of the identity section `e : Spec k → G` (well-defined as a
217	single point of `|G|` because `Spec k` is a topological singleton). -/
218	private noncomputable def identitySectionPoint
219	    {k : Type u} [Field k] (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] : G.left :=
220	  ((MonObj.one (X := G)).left.base :
221	      ↥(Spec (.of k)) → G.left) (default : Spec (.of k))
222	
223	/-- Helper (iter-186; iter-187 closed): the **clopen carrier `Set`** of
224	the identity component of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type,
225	packaged as a `G.left.Opens`. The carrier set is `connectedComponent x`
226	for `x = e(*)` the image of the identity section
227	(`identitySectionPoint G`); openness is `isOpen_connectedComponent` which
228	needs the `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance above
229	(EGA I 6.1.9). -/
230	private noncomputable def identityComponentCarrier {k : Type u} [Field k]
231	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
232	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
233	    G.left.Opens :=
234	  ⟨connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G), isOpen_connectedComponent⟩
235	
236	/-- The **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
237	finite type.
238	
239	Encoded as a `k`-scheme: an object of `Over (Spec (.of k))`, carrying the
240	intended substantive identity "this is the connected component of `|G|`
241	containing the identity section `e`, equipped with the open-subscheme
242	structure inherited from `G`". The associated open immersion
243	`IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` is the content of
244	`IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`.
245	
246	iter-186 body: built from the `identityComponentCarrier G` helper — the
247	open subscheme of `G` whose underlying topological space is the connected
248	component of `|G|` through the image of the identity section. The
249	structure morphism is the inherited `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom`.
250	The substantive content (the actual carrier `Set`, plus its openness from
251	EGA I 6.1.9: locally Noetherian spaces have open connected components)
252	lives in the typed-sorry body of `identityComponentCarrier`. -/
253	noncomputable def IdentityComponent {k : Type u} [Field k]
254	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
255	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
256	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
257	  Over.mk ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)
258	
259	/-- **The identity component is an open and closed subgroup scheme.**
260	
261	The bundled statement of Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3): the identity
262	component `G^0 = IdentityComponent G` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
263	finite type comes with a morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` (in
264	`Over (Spec (.of k))`) whose underlying scheme morphism is both an open
265	immersion and a closed immersion (i.e. the inclusion of a clopen
266	subscheme).
267	
268	The full Kleiman conclusion also packages the group-subscheme property (the
269	inclusion is a homomorphism of `k`-group schemes), finite-type-ness over `k`
270	(`LocallyOfFiniteType` + quasi-compactness), geometric irreducibility, and
271	base-change-commutation. Those refinements live as separate instances /
272	follow-up lemmas in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the clopen
273	open-immersion conclusion as a Nonempty-witness.
274	
275	iter-186 body: the inclusion morphism is `Over.homMk (identityComponentCarrier G).ι`,
276	with the over-category compatibility holding by definition of
277	`IdentityComponent G`. The `.left` of this morphism is
278	`(identityComponentCarrier G).ι`, an open immersion by the global
279	`Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι` instance. For the closed-immersion
280	half we apply `IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion` to the open immersion
281	and reduce to `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G) : Set _)`. The
282	remaining inline `sorry` is the substantive content: the carrier of
283	`identityComponentCarrier G` is closed, by "closure of a connected subspace
284	is connected" applied to the connected component through the image of the
285	identity section. -/
286	theorem IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
287	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
288	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
289	    Nonempty {f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G //
290	        IsOpenImmersion f.left ∧ IsClosedImmersion f.left} := by
291	  -- Over-category compatibility for the underlying open immersion.
292	  have hcomp : (identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom = (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
293	    simp [IdentityComponent]
294	  -- The over-morphism witness.
295	  let f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G :=
296	    Over.homMk (U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G) (identityComponentCarrier G).ι hcomp
297	  refine ⟨⟨f, ?_, ?_⟩⟩
298	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` (definitionally; `Over.homMk_left` simp).
299	    -- An open immersion by `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι`.
300	    change IsOpenImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
301	    infer_instance
302	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` is a preimmersion (open immersion ⟹
303	    -- immersion ⟹ preimmersion). Its range is the carrier set (`Scheme.Opens.range_ι`),
304	    -- and closure of that connected subset is itself (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3):
305	    -- the connected component through the identity is closed in `|G|`).
306	    change IsClosedImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
307	    apply IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion
308	    rw [Scheme.Opens.range_ι]
309	    -- The carrier set is `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`, which is
310	    -- closed by `isClopen_connectedComponent.1` (with the
311	    -- `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance providing the closedness
312	    -- half of EGA I 6.1.9: connected components of a locally Noetherian topological
313	    -- space are clopen).
314	    change IsClosed (connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G))
315	    exact isClopen_connectedComponent.1

382	/-- **Stacks 04KU / EGA IV₂ 4.5.14**: a connected `k`-scheme with a `k`-rational
383	section is geometrically connected.
384	
385	Given a morphism `f : X ⟶ Spec k` from a `ConnectedSpace`-typed scheme `X`
386	admitting a section `s : Spec k ⟶ X` (i.e. `s ≫ f = 𝟙`), the morphism `f` is
387	geometrically connected: for any field extension `K/k`, the pullback
388	`X ×_{Spec k} Spec K` is connected.
389	
390	iter-193 Lane A.3.i, planner-set authorisation per `PROGRESS.md`: ships as a
391	typed `sorry` to enable downstream consumers (sanctioned temporary sorry-count

393	`X_K = U ⊔ V` would, via the base-changed section `s_K = s ≫ (pullback π s)⁻¹`

395	chosen piece under the projection `X_K → X` would then be a clopen subset

405	`F ⊆ Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` containing `k` maps injectively into `k` (as `F` is a field),

478	  -- `MonObj.one.left ≫ G.hom = (𝟙_).hom = 𝟙 (Spec k)` (defeq).

500	private instance identityComponent_geometricallyConnected

678	/-! ## §3. The degree map
679	
680	The disjoint-union structure of `Pic_{C/k}` (a disjoint union of open
681	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via
682	`PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient`) stratifies its `T`-points by the leading
683	coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
684	relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation. On `k`-points this gives the
685	**degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
686	
687	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree_pic` (Milne III.1, p.~88). -/
688	
689	namespace PicScheme
690	
691	/-- The **degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
692	
693	Sends a `k`-point `λ ∈ Pic_{C/k}(k)` --- a morphism
694	`Spec k ⟶ (PicScheme C).left` --- to the leading coefficient of the
695	Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf `L` on `C` (relative
696	to a fixed degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`). By Riemann--Roch,
697	`χ(C, L ⊗ O_C(n)) = n · deg L + 1 - g`, so the degree is the leading
698	coefficient of `Φ_L(n)`, well-defined on the isomorphism class `[L]` and on
699	the `k`-point `λ` (because `PicScheme C` represents the étale-sheafified

700	relative Picard functor).
701	
702	The degree map is a group homomorphism for the additive structure on
703	`Pic_{C/k}(k)` (tensor product on `L`) and the standard `(ℤ, +)`. The full
704	group-homomorphism refinement / functoriality in `k` lives as a follow-up
705	lemma in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the underlying function.
706	
707	iter-186+: the body extracts the representing invertible sheaf from
708	`(PicScheme.representable C)`, forms its Hilbert polynomial via the project's
709	Hilbert-polynomial machinery (sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`), and returns
710	the leading coefficient. For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed
711	`sorry`. -/

723	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies `Pic⁰_{C/k}` with an
724	abelian variety of dimension `g(C)` --- the Jacobian variety of `C`. In
725	this project, "abelian variety" is the conjunction of the four properties
726	`[GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]`
727	threaded through `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity` and consumed by

738	over a field `k`, the identity component `Pic⁰_{C/k}` of the Picard scheme
739	is an *abelian variety over* `k`: a smooth, proper, geometrically
740	irreducible `k`-group scheme. (Commutativity follows automatically from
741	Milne §I.1, Cor. 1.4, and is not separately stated here.)

743	The bundled statement: the four pieces `[IsProper] ∧ [Smooth] ∧
744	[GeometricallyIrreducible] ∧ Nonempty (GrpObj _)` hold on
745	`(Pic0Scheme C).hom` and `(Pic0Scheme C)` respectively.

747	Dimension `g = g(C)`: not stated separately at the file-skeleton level

753	- `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme` (clopen subscheme of
754	  `PicScheme C`),
755	- Kleiman §5 Thm.~`th:qpp&p` (`X/k` projective + geom. integral ⟹
756	  `Pic⁰_{X/k}` quasi-projective; upgrade to projective when geom. normal,

758	- Kleiman §5 Cor.~`cor:sm` + Ex.~`ex:jac` (smoothness of `Pic_{X/k}` at the
759	  identity for smooth proper curves; hence smooth of dimension
760	  `dim_k H¹(C, O_C) = g(C)` everywhere by uniform smoothness on the open
761	  identity component).

765	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
766	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :

769	      Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C)) :=
770	  sorry

773	
774	Milne~§III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): "The dimension of `J` is the genus of `C`".
775	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k` of genus
776	`g = g(C)`, the topological Krull dimension of the underlying scheme of
777	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` equals `g(C)`. By Kleiman~§5 Cor.~`cor:sm`, the inequality
778	`dim Pic_{C/k} ≤ dim_k H¹(C, O_C)` is always an equality at points where

11	
12	The genus of a smooth, proper, geometrically irreducible curve over a field.
13	
14	## Status (closed iteration 011 — `genus`)
15	
16	The iter-011 refactor added the `noncomputable` modifier (user-authorised
17	`2026-05-07`) and an import of `AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK`
18	so the body uses the project's `ModuleCat k`-flavoured cohomology
19	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009) of the structure sheaf
20	`Scheme.toModuleKSheaf` (iter-006). The body is the probe-confirmed
21	one-liner
22	
23	    Module.finrank k (Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1)
24	
25	which is the honest mathematical definition `dim_k H^1(C, O_C)`.

27	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Genus.tex` for the informal proof sketch and
28	`.archon/STRATEGY.md` for the multi-phase build-out plan.

41	    [IsProper C.hom] [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
42	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] : ℕ :=

/-
